item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I like how unapologetic this entry is in its simplicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the central idea and the absurd horror of the situation, but this just doesn't do enough with any of the concepts for me to enjoy it fully.

As others have said, it's all fairly straightforward and doesn't add any interesting twists or consequences: things just sort of progress from one event to another without any real work done to give us payoff. Broken Masquerade is huge. London being essentially destroyed is huge. The Foundation losing UK's trust is huge.

But you didn't do anything with those interesting plot threads: instead the ending is a non-sequitur with the giant hand and pin, which also could have potential but in absence of context just comes off as goofy.

This all feels mostly like background information, like I'm waiting for the actual conflict and story to show up. If you have more in mind for this story, it would be great to know what comes next, as I feel this isn't complete yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know about this one.

I would not come on as strongly negative as some other commenters have, but I feel like this largely just isn't that interesting. It's not funny enough to be funny and it's not scary enough to be horrifying , and "what if someone's head just started expanding, dude" is not really that compelling a premise on its own to me. The pin pop at the end is like, mildly amusing I guess, but this doesn't leave me with any strong feelings and more damningly I'm not really asking any questions either.

I think my big gripe with this one is that it just doesn't seem to have a purpose beyond being a reference to a Jimmy Neutron episode I've never seen (and I wouldn't even have known that without reading the comments). Pop culture can be a compelling starting point for an SCP but this just doesn't do anything for me.

-1 for the time being. There are ways this could be vastly improved but it'd sort of necessitate a wholesale rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, I will have to watch Jimmy Neutron to understand what's going on. Only if I wasn't such an uncultured swine who had a boring childhood…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you made an SCP out of a jimmy neutron episode. that takes guts.

unfortunately this never really hooked me like it could have? it feels like the timeline of events has some extraneous bits in it. also adapting stuff from IRL pop culture into SCPs is still blase, isn't it?

-1 but has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate myself for recognizing the name "Sheen Estevez" and remembering the whole episode without even having to google it. Nostalgia is going to be the death of me…. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really skeptical at the start of reading but I really like this now. I love how the scip takes a really silly idea and lets it play out realistically (or at least as realistic as one can expect from the SCP universe). Also, this kinda a controversial opinion but I really like the literal deus ex machina ending. It's the absurd cherry on top of this ice cream sundae of silliness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reference is a little on-the-nose for my taste (even though it's an essential part of the joke), but I think laughing and feeling the hibbie jibbies at the same time is quite the feeling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s comedic, yes, but also signifies how fragile the veil is. Any random event could mean the end of normalcy. +1

Exactly my thought. That's also the reason of why this takes the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm unsure about the ending, but the fact there's no explanation actually makes it better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ehhh, the giant hand with pin and that kinda ruined it for me plus no explanation on what cause it in the first pace -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SHEEN NOOOOOOO

Ok I really wanna know more about that hand and needle though, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly agree with the negative critique of Tufto and Kirby.

I also really dislike the literal deux ex machina that resolves the issue (I get the joke/how it's supposed the funny, I just personally think it's not funny, more of an anti climax). Also, I dislike how the skip seems to underplay the massive consequences of a Broken Masquerade event - surely in-universe the document of a BM-scenario-causing skip would contain courses of action or comments on it beyond a timeline. Neat premise though.

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the straightforwardness of this. Not every article has to be some grand 9,000 word literary achievement or go into deep lore of the expanded universe. Every now and then it's nice to see a straightforward piece that's just about some weird shit happening and science guys trying to cover it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1230 The American ambassador is discovered looking in on the procession with comically over-sized binoculars. Upon being discovered, he attempts to run down the block, only to be crushed by a 160cm3 anvil weight, complete with "1 POUND" written in white paint.
1231 The anvil is then squashed, with the appearance of crumpling paper, by a large dismembered foot falling from the sky. Witness accounts describe the sound similar to a person "blowing a raspberry"
1232 Deafening fanfare blankets London. The clouds part, and colossal, red, floating letters emerge in the sky spelling out "MONTY PYTHONS FLYING CIRCUS"

And now for something completely different.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The juxtaposition of the horror/real implications of the silly premise are entirely undercut by the entirely uninteresting and drab narrative we're actually presented with. It's an interesting premise but nothing's done with it beyond, as Kirby says, an entirely generic and predictable timeline of events. Strong -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This honestly came off as rather scary to me. Imagine being in an overseer’s shoes. You’re at the head of the biggest conspiracy on Earth, containing horrific monsters, primordial malevolent forces, and eldritch abominations, performing horrible acts to do so. Kids are sacrificed in flames to contain the DEER. People’s memories are treated like playthings to appease Anantashesha. Numerous D-class are disposed of and sent to an unknown fate to hopefully redirect Khevtuul 1. Yet, the thing that finally breaks the veil isn’t something planned, isn’t something within anyone’s control. A head simply starts to expand.

It’s comedic, yes, but also signifies how fragile the veil is. Any random event could mean the end of normalcy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest gripe with this is definitely the timeline. Largely because, while I understand that it effectively works as a way to build tension for the ending, the events in the timeline itself feel very straight forward. Given the fact that we know how the timeline ends, I didn’t feel like the foundation’s response was specifically tailored to the anomaly (which sort of makes sense because the mechanics of the anomaly means that nothing can really be done). But because of this, the timeline events were either descriptions of the head expanding (which isn’t particularly new information given that we know this is what was going to happen from the beginning), or somewhat generic foundation responses (calling in an mtf, suppressing information, debating nuclear strikes, etc).

I feel like I would have enjoyed this more if you didn’t lead with the fact that the brain gets this large and explodes. Largely because the moment you say that, I have an idea in my head of what the timeline is going to look like. Once I have this idea in my head, the only thing that surprised me was the ending. But if you never mentioned the fact that the brain gets big enough to cause a BM scenario, or explode at all, and just assert that it expands, then that would have allowed you to explore the dramatic escalation without telegraphing a lot of it to the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ending definitely caught me off guard; would've assumed this was a Shaggy Dog jamcon entry if I didn't know better. I think what elevates this beyond a simple bait-and-switch is how it culminates in a broken masquerade and mass casualties, but the tone and structure of the article play it off as an anticlimax. The source of the resolution probably ought to feel non-sequitur and contrived, but the imagery is so evocative of Terry Gilliam (helped by the British setting) that it just feels right somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Funniest skip i've ever read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After I read this, my head exploded instantly. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deliciously simple. +1

item.title: Mfw
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: real galaxy brain hours who up click +1

item.title: シン・ゴジラ
item.content:encoded:
"No Carl, you don't understand. Sheen's brain is programmed to keep growing. If we don't get that Brain Drain Helmet on him soon, his head will explode." - Jimmy Neutron

I blame a late-night Waffle House run for this one. Special thanks, as usual, to The Great Hippo for his editing and critique, as well as to Vezaz for his critique and UraniumEmpire for her suggestions to flesh out the timeline more. Also thanks to magnadeus, Uncle Nicolini, MaliceAforethought, and ch00bakka for their feedback.

Finally, shoutout to psul and SCP-2365 for inspiring the ending.
